name: Cassandra
description: Cassandra, daughter of King Priam and Queen Hecuba of Troy, was blessed with the gift of prophecy by the god Apollo, who desired her. However, when she rejected his advances, Apollo cursed her so that her prophecies, while accurate, would never be believed. This curse became her tragic fate. Throughout the Trojan War, Cassandra foresaw the destruction of Troy, the treachery of the Trojan Horse, and the deaths of many, including her own family members. Despite her desperate warnings, no one heeded her words, dismissing her as mad. She witnessed the fall of Troy, helpless to prevent the inevitable. After the sack of the city, she sought sanctuary in the temple of Athena, but was brutally raped by Ajax the Lesser and taken as a war prize by Agamemnon. She foretold his murder at the hands of his wife Clytemnestra and her lover Aegisthus, and ultimately met her own demise alongside Agamemnon in Mycenae. Cassandra represents the tragic figure of the misunderstood prophet, whose knowledge is both a gift and a curse, highlighting the futility of truth when it is ignored or disbelieved. Her story is a powerful commentary on the dangers of hubris and the consequences of rejecting wisdom.
subtitle: The cursed prophetess of Troy
achievements: Received the gift of prophecy from Apollo
Became a symbol of unheeded truth

keyMyths.title: Cassandra's Gift and Curse
keyMyths.description: Apollo granted Cassandra the power of prophecy but cursed her so no one would believe her after she rejected him. This cruel twist defined her life and made her a tragic figure.
keyMyths.source: Various Greek tragedies and accounts of the Trojan War

keyMyths.title: Warning Against the Trojan Horse
keyMyths.description: Cassandra vehemently warned the Trojans against accepting the Trojan Horse, knowing it was a trick that would lead to their downfall. Sadly, her warnings were ignored.

keyMyths.title: Death in Mycenae
keyMyths.description: Taken as a slave by Agamemnon, Cassandra foresaw and was murdered alongside him by Clytemnestra and Aegisthus upon their return to Mycenae. Her death fulfilled her final prophecy.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides glimpses of Cassandra and the Trojan War.
sources.text: Provides glimpses of Cassandra and the Trojan War.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: Depicts Cassandra's arrival in Mycenae and her prophetic death.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Details Cassandra's warnings about the Trojan Horse.
